message.content:
You are an INDEPENDENT verifier adjudicating audit finding GitHub issue #7 for /Users/thomasjones/hc-assay. You did NOT author it. TRUE_POSITIVE vs FALSE_POSITIVE; try HARD to refute. Run repros with /Users/thomasjones/hc-assay/.venv/bin/python. READ-ONLY: no edits, no git, no gh.

FINDING (#7, stated severity MEDIUM): The frozen dataclasses carrying Mapping/dict fields (Verdict, Interpretation/Measurement, Hypothesis, Unit/Relation/Corpus, ClaimRecord, BaselineArtifact, etc.) have two defects: (a) unhashable despite frozen=True — hash() raises TypeError because of the contained dict, though frozen makes them look hashable; (b) shallow immutability — the contained dict is mutable in place, so "frozen" provenance objects can be mutated post-construction.

Independently verify: repro hash(Verdict("h", VerdictLabel.SUPPORTED, "r")) and u=Unit("b"); u.attributes["x"]=1. Consider counter-arguments (are these objects ever actually hashed/used as keys in the codebase? does it matter in practice yet?). Assess MEDIUM severity (maybe lower if purely latent).

Return EXACTLY:
VERDICT: TRUE_POSITIVE | FALSE_POSITIVE | PARTIAL
CONFIDENCE: high | medium | low
SEVERITY_ASSESSMENT: <...>
REASONING: <your own evidence>
FIX_GUIDANCE: <...>

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

src/assay_engine/methodology/hypothesis.py:36:class Hypothesis:

1	"""The three verdicts (ADR-0004, METHODOLOGY.md §4).
2	
3	A confirmatory test returns exactly one of supported / contradicted / indeterminate — never
4	a forced binary. Every verdict carries the evidence and the decision rule behind it, so a
5	reviewer can re-derive it.
6	"""
7	
8	from __future__ import annotations
9	
10	from dataclasses import dataclass, field
11	from enum import Enum
12	from typing import Any, Mapping
13	
14	
15	class VerdictLabel(Enum):
16	    SUPPORTED = "supported"
17	    CONTRADICTED = "contradicted"
18	    INDETERMINATE = "indeterminate"
19	
20	
21	@dataclass(frozen=True, slots=True)
22	class Verdict:
23	    """The outcome of one confirmatory test, structured identically for all three labels.
24	
25	    ``indeterminate`` is first-class: underpowered, beyond the measurement's reach, or a
26	    disagreement plausibly attributable to a method limitation rather than a claim error.
27	    """
28	
29	    hypothesis_id: str
30	    label: VerdictLabel
31	    decision_rule: str
32	    statistic: float | None = None
33	    threshold: float | None = None
34	    evidence: Mapping[str, Any] = field(default_factory=dict)
35	    notes: str = ""
36	
37	    @property
38	    def is_indeterminate(self) -> bool:
39	        return self.label is VerdictLabel.INDETERMINATE
40	
41	    @classmethod
42	    def supported(cls, hypothesis_id: str, decision_rule: str, **kw: Any) -> "Verdict":
43	        return cls(hypothesis_id, VerdictLabel.SUPPORTED, decision_rule, **kw)
44	
45	    @classmethod
46	    def contradicted(cls, hypothesis_id: str, decision_rule: str, **kw: Any) -> "Verdict":
47	        return cls(hypothesis_id, VerdictLabel.CONTRADICTED, decision_rule, **kw)
48	
49	    @classmethod
50	    def indeterminate(cls, hypothesis_id: str, decision_rule: str, **kw: Any) -> "Verdict":
51	        return cls(hypothesis_id, VerdictLabel.INDETERMINATE, decision_rule, **kw)
52

1	"""Canonical schema — the dataset-agnostic shape the baseline is built from.
2	
3	An adapter's job is to turn its raw source into a :class:`Corpus` of these types. The engine
4	builds the baseline from a ``Corpus`` and never sees the source's native format.
5	
6	Firewall A boundary (critical):
7	    A ``Corpus`` carries the *data itself* — units of text/attributes and any structure that
8	    is intrinsic to the raw source. It must **not** carry an external authority's *judgments*
9	    about the data (asserted relationships, strengths, labels, a taxonomy). Those are claims;
10	    they travel through :mod:`assay_engine.contracts.claims`, quarantined from baseline
11	    construction. If an adapter is tempted to put an external mapping into ``Corpus.relations``,
12	    that is a Firewall A violation — it belongs in the claims source instead.
13	"""
14	
15	from __future__ import annotations
16	
17	from dataclasses import dataclass, field
18	from typing import Any, Mapping
19	
20	
21	@dataclass(frozen=True, slots=True)
22	class Unit:
23	    """One analyzable object in the corpus (a document, record, entity, item).
24	
25	    ``unit_id`` is stable and unique within the corpus. ``text`` is the natural-language
26	    content the NLP baseline operates on (may be empty for non-text units). ``attributes``
27	    holds structured fields intrinsic to the data — never an external authority's verdicts.
28	    """
29	
30	    unit_id: str
31	    text: str = ""
32	    attributes: Mapping[str, Any] = field(default_factory=dict)
33	    source_ref: str | None = None
34	
35	
36	@dataclass(frozen=True, slots=True)
37	class Relation:
38	    """A relationship that is *intrinsic to the raw data* (not an external judgment).
39	
40	    Example of legitimate use: a parent/child link the source file itself encodes. Example of
41	    illegitimate use: an external expert's asserted mapping between units — that is a claim
42	    (see :mod:`assay_engine.contracts.claims`) and must be withheld from the baseline.
43	    """
44	
45	    source_id: str
46	    target_id: str
47	    kind: str
48	    attributes: Mapping[str, Any] = field(default_factory=dict)
49	
50	
51	@dataclass(frozen=True, slots=True)
52	class Corpus:
53	    """The full canonical dataset handed to the baseline builder."""
54	
55	    units: tuple[Unit, ...]
56	    relations: tuple[Relation, ...] = ()
57	    metadata: Mapping[str, Any] = field(default_factory=dict)
58	
59	    def unit_ids(self) -> frozenset[str]:
60	        return frozenset(u.unit_id for u in self.units)
61	
62	    def __post_init__(self) -> None:
63	        ids = [u.unit_id for u in self.units]
64	        if len(ids) != len(set(ids)):
65	            raise ValueError("Corpus contains duplicate unit_id values")
66

content.text: Now let me run the repros and check whether these objects are ever hashed or used as keys.

input.command:
cd /Users/thomasjones/hc-assay && .venv/bin/python -c "
from assay_engine.methodology.verdict import Verdict, VerdictLabel
from assay_engine.contracts.schema import Unit

# Repro (a): hash should raise TypeError
v = Verdict('h', VerdictLabel.SUPPORTED, 'r')
print('frozen?', type(v).__dataclass_params__.frozen)
try:
    h = hash(v)
    print('hash OK:', h)
except TypeError as e:
    print('hash TypeError:', e)

# Repro (b): shallow mutation
u = Unit('b')
print('before:', dict(u.attributes))
u.attributes['x'] = 1
print('after mutation:', dict(u.attributes))
print('frozen blocks attr assignment?')
try:
    u.text = 'changed'
except Exception as e:
    print('  attr assign blocked:', type(e).__name__)
"
input.description: Run repros for hash and mutation

content.text: Both defects confirmed. Now let me check whether these objects are actually hashed or used as dict keys / set members anywhere in the codebase.

content.text: That single hit is `frozenset` over `unit_id` strings, not over `Unit` objects. So none of these objects are hashed or used as keys in the source. Let me check tests and whether mutation actually happens anywhere, plus check the other dataclasses for the same pattern.

content.text: Both defects are real and reproduce exactly as stated. But neither object is hashed nor mutated anywhere in the codebase today — purely latent. Let me confirm the standard `frozen=True` behavior nuance to characterize precisely (frozen without eq=False still sets `__hash__`).

input.command:
cd /Users/thomasjones/hc-assay && .venv/bin/python -c "
from assay_engine.methodology.verdict import Verdict
print('__hash__ is None?', Verdict.__hash__ is None)
print('__hash__:', Verdict.__hash__)
# So dataclass GENERATED a __hash__, but it fails at runtime because dict is unhashable.
# This is the 'looks hashable but isn't' trap.
"

content.text:
Confirmed the precise mechanism. Both claims are verified.

VERDICT: TRUE_POSITIVE
CONFIDENCE: high
SEVERITY_ASSESSMENT: LOW (downgrade from MEDIUM). Both defects are real and verifiable, but purely latent — neither is triggered by any current code path.

REASONING:
Both halves of the finding reproduce exactly with the project venv:

(a) Unhashable-despite-frozen. `Verdict` (and the other `frozen=True` dataclasses) carries `__dataclass_params__.frozen == True` and dataclass generated a real `__hash__` (`Verdict.__hash__ is None` -> False; it's a live function). But `hash(Verdict("h", VerdictLabel.SUPPORTED, "r"))` raises `TypeError: unhashable type: 'dict'` because the generated `__hash__` hashes the field tuple, which includes the `evidence` dict. This is the "looks hashable but isn't" trap the finding describes: API surface advertises hashability, runtime denies it.

(b) Shallow immutability. `u = Unit("b"); u.attributes["x"] = 1` mutates the supposedly-frozen object's state in place (before: `{}`, after: `{'x': 1}`). `frozen=True` only blocks attribute rebinding (`u.text = ...` -> `FrozenInstanceError`), not mutation of the contained `dict`. So "frozen" provenance objects can be silently altered post-construction.

Both patterns are confirmed across the broader set: 8 modules define `frozen=True` dataclasses with `Mapping[...]` fields (claims.py, schema.py, fence.py, hypothesis.py, verdict.py, toolkit.py, seam.py, gates.py).

Counter-arguments (why severity is LOW, not MEDIUM):
- Not actually hashed anywhere. The only `frozenset`/`set`/`hash` usage over these types is `Corpus.unit_ids()` which hashes `unit_id` strings, not `Unit` objects. No source or test uses any of these dataclasses as a dict key or set member. The TypeError is never hit today.
- Not actually mutated anywhere. Grep for in-place `.attributes[...]=` / `.evidence[...]=` / `.metadata[...]=` assignments across src/ and tests/ returns zero hits. The mutation is possible but not exercised.
- So this is a latent correctness/robustness defect, not an active bug. It bites only when someone later puts a `Verdict`/`Unit`/etc. in a set or as a dict key (a natural thing to do for dedup/provenance keying — exactly the use these immutable records invite), or relies on "frozen" to mean deeply immutable. Given the codebase's explicit framing (provenance, Firewall A boundary, "frozen" provenance objects), the immutability gap is the more meaningful half. PARTIAL was considered but rejected: both stated claims are factually true; only the severity is overstated.

FIX_GUIDANCE:
- Replace the mutable `Mapping[str, Any] = field(default_factory=dict)` fields with a genuinely immutable mapping at construction time. Practical options:
  1. Normalize in `__post_init__` via `object.__setattr__(self, "evidence", MappingProxyType(dict(evidence)))` (works with frozen by bypassing the frozen setter), giving a read-only view that rejects item assignment. Wrap a copy so callers can't mutate via their original reference.
  2. Or use `frozendict`/an immutable-mapping type as the field type, converting in `__post_init__`.
- For hashability: a `MappingProxyType` is still unhashable, so if these objects should be set/dict keys, also provide a `__hash__` based only on the stable scalar identity fields (e.g. `hypothesis_id`/`label` for `Verdict`, `unit_id` for `Unit`) via `eq=True` plus a custom `__hash__`, or freeze the mapping into a hashable form (`tuple(sorted(...))`). If they should NOT be keys, set `eq=False` or `unsafe_hash`-avoid and document non-hashability rather than leaving a `__hash__` that raises.
- Apply uniformly across all 8 modules listed; a shared helper (e.g. a `freeze_mapping` used in each `__post_init__`) keeps the boundary consistent.
